solution.p: We will factor 9r^{2}-121 where a=9, b=0, and c=-121.

Difference of Squares Formula: The formula for a difference of squares is a^2-b^2 = (a+b)(a-b) Note that the polynomial 9r^{2}-121 matches the formula's right side, and we have: \begin{aligned}[t]9r^{2}-121\amp =\left(3r\right)^2-\left(11\right)^2\newline \amp =\left(3r+11\right)\mathopen{}\left(3r-11\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (9) \cdot (-121) = -1089.

The number -1089 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(-1089)\amp -1(1089)\amp 3(-363)\amp -3(363)
ewline9(-121)\amp -9(121)\amp 11(-99)\amp -11(99)
ewline33(-33)\amp -33(33)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 0, and this sum happens with (-33)+33=0 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]9r^{2}-121\amp =9r^{2}-33r+33r-121
ewline \amp =3r\mathopen{}\left(3r-11ight)+11\mathopen{}\left(3r-11ight)
ewline \amp =\left(3r+11ight)\mathopen{}\left(3r-11ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in 9r^{2}-121 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 9, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(9)\amp 3(3)\amp \end{array}
The third term, -121, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(-121)\amp -1(121)\amp 11(-11)\amp -11(11)\newline\end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with 9r^{2}-121 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]9r^{2}-121\amp =\left(3r+11\right)\mathopen{}\left(3r-11\right)\end{aligned}
